questions.question: What is the purpose of the 'super' keyword in Java?
options: A) To access class variables
B) To override a parent class method
C) To call parent class constructor
D) To create a new class
questions.explanation: The 'super' keyword in Java is used to call a parent class constructor from a child class constructor.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a multilevel inheritance?
options: A) Animal -> Mammal
B) Animal -> Dog
C) Mammal -> Human
D) Animal -> Cat
questions.explanation: Multilevel inheritance is when a child class inherits from a parent class, and the parent class itself inherits from another class.

questions.question: What happens when you create a constructor in a child class without calling the superclass constructor?
options: A) It creates a new instance of the superclass
B) It calls the superclass constructor
C) It hides the superclass constructor
D) It throws an error
questions.explanation: When a child class constructor is not explicitly called, the superclass constructor is implicitly called.

questions.question: Can a child class have multiple superclass constructors?
options: A) Yes, but only one at a time
B) No, but multiple in the same time
C) Yes, and they can be called in any order
D) No, never
questions.explanation: A child class can have multiple superclass constructors, but they must be called in the order they appear in the child class constructor.

questions.question: What is the difference between 'this' and 'super' in a constructor?
options: A) 'this' refers to the parent class, 'super' refers to the child class
B) 'this' refers to the child class, 'super' refers to the parent class
C) 'this' refers to the current object, 'super' refers to the superclass
D) 'this' refers to the superclass, 'super' refers to the child class
questions.explanation: In a constructor, 'this' refers to the current object being created, while 'super' refers to the superclass constructor being called.
